title: feat: scaffold the Astro landing page — hero, the loop, quickstart, docs links

acceptanceCriteria.text: `npm run build` produces `dist/index.html` from a clean checkout.
The page contains: the pitch line, the loop with the four gates, the quickstart steps, and working links to the parent repo docs.
`node --test` is green and actually exercises the build (fails if the hero content is removed).
No dependencies beyond `astro` itself.

issue.body:
## Goal

A deployable first cut of the landing page: an Astro static site with a hero that pitches the protocol, the read → work → verify → write loop, a quickstart install block, and links to the source-of-truth docs in `chalkagents/chalk-protocol`.

## Scope

- Astro project at the repo root: `npm run dev` / `npm run build` → static output in `dist/`.
- Single page (`src/pages/index.astro`) with these sections:
  - **Hero** — name, one-line pitch ("a layer-2 harness for coding agents: the agent never self-declares done"), `npm install -g chalk-protocol` (or the actual install command from the parent README), link to GitHub.
  - **The loop** — read → work → verify → write, one line per gate (verify gate, locked tests, adversarial review, held-out audit).
  - **Quickstart** — the 4-step `chalk init` → `chalk spec` → `chalk verify` → `chalk done` flow.
  - **Docs links** — PROTOCOL.md, QUICKSTART.md, RESEARCH.md on the parent repo (build-time markdown rendering is a follow-up issue, not this one).
- Dark, terminal-flavored styling; no client-side JS; no extra dependencies beyond Astro.
- A locked test (`test/site.test.mjs`, plain `node --test`) that installs deps if missing, runs the build, and asserts `dist/index.html` exists and contains the pitch line, the install command, and the GitHub link.

## Non-goals

- Build-time markdown rendering from the parent repo (follow-up).
- Vercel project + domain wiring (follow-up; needs dashboard access).
- Analytics, OG images, multi-page docs.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `npm run build` produces `dist/index.html` from a clean checkout.
- [ ] The page contains: the pitch line, the loop with the four gates, the quickstart steps, and working links to the parent repo docs.
- [ ] `node --test` is green and actually exercises the build (fails if the hero content is removed).
- [ ] No dependencies beyond `astro` itself.

findings.note: Generated `.astro/` artifacts (content.d.ts, content-assets.mjs, types.d.ts) are committed even though they are build-time output and reference a non-existent `src/content.config.mjs`. Astro regenerates these on build so nothing breaks, but they are stale noise that would confuse `astro check`/editor TS and should be gitignored like dist/.

test/build.test.mjs `before` hook runs `npm install` when node_modules is absent, making the test depend on network access on a clean checkout. Acceptable given criterion 1 requires building from a clean checkout, but a registry outage will report as a test failure rather than a build defect.

Criterion 2 requires 'working links to the parent repo docs'. The test only asserts the href strings (README.md, PROTOCOL.md, QUICKSTART.md, RESEARCH.md) exist in the HTML; nothing verifies those files actually exist in chalkagents/chalk-protocol (e.g., RESEARCH.md). Link liveness is unverifiable offline, so this is noted, not blocking.

Adequacy is otherwise solid: the suite rebuilds on every run and asserts dist/index.html, the exact pitch line (fails if hero content is removed), all four gate names, the quickstart chalk commands, all four doc URLs, and that package.json's only dependency is astro — so reverting the change makes the suite fail, satisfying the non-vacuous-coverage rule.

plan: Repo surveyed: it's an empty scaffold — only `.chalk/`, `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`. No `package.json`, no Astro, no tests. Verify runs `node --test` only (no separate build lane), so the test itself must drive the build. ## Plan **Approach:** Hand-write a minimal Astro scaffold (package.json + astro.config.mjs + one `index.astro` page) rather than `npm create astro` (which drags in templates/tsconfig noise and risks extra deps). The `node --test` file shells out to `npm run build` then asserts on `dist/index.html` content — this is the only way criterion 3 ("test exercises the build") is met given verify's single `node --test` lane. **Steps:** 1. Create `package.json`: name `chalk-protocol-site`, `"type": "module"`, scripts `dev: astro dev`, `build: astro build`, `test: node --test`; sole dependency `astro` (pinned `^5`). Run of `npm install` is executor's local concern; commit `package-lock.json` for clean-checkout reproducibility. 2. Create `astro.config.mjs` with `defineConfig({ site: 'https://protocol.chalkagents.com' })`. 3. Create `src/pages/index.astro` — single self-contained page, inline `

title: feat: rebuild-on-upstream-change — Vercel deploy hook triggered from chalk-protocol CI

acceptanceCriteria.text: Editing a rendered doc on chalk-protocol main triggers a production rebuild here within minutes.
No secret material lands in either repo's git history; hook secret lives only in chalk-protocol's Actions secrets.

issue.body:
## Goal

The site rebuilds when `chalkagents/chalk-protocol` changes, so build-time-rendered docs stay fresh without manual redeploys.

## Scope

- Create a **Vercel Deploy Hook** (unauthenticated secret URL) for this project's production branch.
- Store it as an Actions secret **in the parent repo** (`chalk-protocol`), NOT here — this repo stays secretless.
- Parent-repo workflow (small PR to `chalkagents/chalk-protocol`): on push to `main` touching `README.md|PROTOCOL.md|QUICKSTART.md|RESEARCH.md`, `curl -X POST "$SITE_DEPLOY_HOOK"`. Explicit `permissions: contents: read`; the secret is never exposed to fork PRs (push-trigger only).
- Alternative considered: `repository_dispatch` + PAT — rejected (a PAT is a broader credential than a single-purpose deploy hook URL).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Editing a rendered doc on chalk-protocol main triggers a production rebuild here within minutes.
- [ ] No secret material lands in either repo's git history; hook secret lives only in chalk-protocol's Actions secrets.

Depends on: Vercel project issue; pairs with the build-time docs rendering issue.

block.reason: needs a Vercel Deploy Hook URL from the dashboard, stored as an Actions secret in chalk-protocol (this repo stays secretless)

title: feat: build-time docs rendering from chalk-protocol markdown (/protocol, /quickstart, /research)

acceptanceCriteria.text: `/protocol`, `/quickstart`, `/research` render the parent repo's current markdown at build time.
A failed fetch fails `npm run build` (non-zero), it does not ship an empty page.
Landing-page docs links point at the local pages; locked test covers all three pages.

issue.body:
## Goal

Docs pages rendered at build time from the source-of-truth markdown in `chalkagents/chalk-protocol` (README, PROTOCOL.md, QUICKSTART.md, RESEARCH.md) — the site can never drift from the repo.

## Scope

- Build-time fetch from `raw.githubusercontent.com/chalkagents/chalk-protocol/main/...` (public repo — no token, no secrets).
- Render each doc as a page (`/protocol`, `/quickstart`, `/research`) with the site's terminal-dark styling; rewrite relative repo links to absolute GitHub URLs.
- **Fail the build loudly** if a fetch fails (a half-empty docs page shipping silently is worse than a red deploy).
- Markdown rendering via Astro's built-in content pipeline — keep the "no deps beyond astro" property if possible; if a markdown lib is unavoidable, justify it in the PR.
- Locked test: build with the real fetch and assert each docs page contains a known heading from its source doc.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/protocol`, `/quickstart`, `/research` render the parent repo's current markdown at build time.
- [ ] A failed fetch fails `npm run build` (non-zero), it does not ship an empty page.
- [ ] Landing-page docs links point at the local pages; locked test covers all three pages.

findings.note: Criterion 3 explicitly requires a LOCKED test covering all three pages, but the task's locked acceptance tests list is '(none)'. test/docs-pages.test.mjs does cover /protocol, /quickstart, /research, the landing links, and the failed-fetch build failure, but it is not registered as a locked test, so it can be freely weakened or deleted later — the 'locked' clause of the contract is unmet. Register it via the sanctioned path (e.g. chalk amend-spec --test test/docs-pages.test.mjs) before this can pass.

The render tests only assert 'some h1/h2 exists' and a source link back to GitHub; they never compare page content against the fetched upstream markdown. Criterion 1 ('renders the parent repo's CURRENT markdown') would still 'pass' if the loader silently served stale/cached or partially wrong content, as long as any heading rendered. Only the DOCS_BASE_URL failure test ties output to the fetch path at all.

The failed-fetch test and the sandbox build share the repo's node_modules (symlinked), so Astro's content-layer data store under node_modules/.astro is shared between the root build (test/build.test.mjs) and both sandbox builds. --test-concurrency=1 serializes test FILES, but this relies on undocumented file-ordering and shared-cache behavior; a future removal of that flag (or parallel CI shards) reintroduces the race the comment warns about. Consider an isolated cache dir per build instead of a global concurrency clamp.

Generated artifacts (.astro/collections/docs.schema.json, .astro/content.d.ts) are committed as part of the change; these are build outputs Astro regenerates and will churn/drift (content.d.ts already points at src/content.config.js while the source is content.config.ts).

The heading assertion /<h[12][\s>]/ and the slug.toUpperCase() source-link check depend on upstream markdown shape and the PROTOCOL/QUICKSTART/RESEARCH naming convention; an upstream doc starting at h3 or a renamed file makes the suite fail for reasons unrelated to this site's code. Acceptable for a build-time mirror, but worth noting as fragility, and the happy-path build requires live network access to raw.githubusercontent.com.

The root `npm run build` (exercised by the pre-existing locked test test/build.test.mjs and by `chalk verify`) now performs live fetches to raw.githubusercontent.com/chalkagents/chalk-protocol/main. Offline, rate-limited, or upstream-renamed-file scenarios will fail an existing test suite that previously had no network dependency. This is arguably by design (criterion 2 demands fail-hard), but it makes CI availability-coupled to GitHub raw.

The failed-fetch test only exercises the connection-refused path (DOCS_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:1). The non-ok HTTP status branch (res.ok false, e.g. 404 from a moved upstream file) and the empty-body guard in content.config.ts are never directly exercised by any test, though they share the same throw-in-loader failure mechanism proven by the covered case.

Criterion 1's 'parent repo's current markdown' default is verified only by grepping the source text of content.config.ts for the literal URL, not by observing a real fetch. It is, however, indirectly covered end-to-end by the root build in test/build.test.mjs, which builds with DOCS_BASE_URL unset and therefore exercises the default.

The diff commits generated Astro artifacts (.astro/collections/docs.schema.json, .astro/content.d.ts edits) while simultaneously adding .astro/ to .gitignore. Once tracked, gitignore does not untrack them; these generated files will drift from future builds. They should be removed from version control.

Both test/build.test.mjs and test/docs-pages.test.mjs check existsSync(node_modules) and run `npm install` in their before hooks; under node --test's default parallel file execution on a clean checkout, two concurrent installs into the same root could race. Existing pattern, but the new file doubles the exposure.

src/pages/[slug].astro is a top-level catch-all dynamic route. Today it only emits /protocol, /quickstart, /research, but any future top-level static page must be checked for route-priority interaction. No current conflict (only index.astro exists).

plan: You've hit your session limit · resets 7:30pm (Asia/Manila)

title: chore(security): safe Vercel wiring for a public repo — main-only production, fork-approval previews, zero secrets

acceptanceCriteria.text: Vercel project connected to `chalkagents/chalk-protocol-site`, framework=Astro, production branch = `main` ONLY.

Fork safety: "Require authorization for deployments from forked repositories" enabled (member approval before a fork PR gets a preview); no env vars configured at all (the site needs none — if one ever appears, scope it to Production only, never Preview).
Deployment protection for previews if on Pro (password/SSO); otherwise accept public unguessable preview URLs — fine for a marketing site.
Domain: `protocol.chalkagents.com` CNAME → Vercel; `chalk-protocol-site.vercel.app` until DNS is ready.
After the domain is live: set `chalkagents/chalk-protocol`'s repo `homepage` field and add the link to its README (small PR in the parent repo).
Production deploys only from pushes to main; PR previews require member approval for forks.
No env vars / secrets in this repo or exposed to previews.
Site reachable at the production URL.

issue.body:
## Goal

Wire Vercel safely for a PUBLIC repo: production = main only, previews can't be weaponized by fork PRs, no secrets anywhere in the repo.

## Checklist (dashboard work — human)

- [ ] Vercel project connected to `chalkagents/chalk-protocol-site`, framework=Astro, production branch = `main` ONLY.
- [ ] Fork safety: "Require authorization for deployments from forked repositories" enabled (member approval before a fork PR gets a preview); no env vars configured at all (the site needs none — if one ever appears, scope it to Production only, never Preview).
- [ ] Deployment protection for previews if on Pro (password/SSO); otherwise accept public unguessable preview URLs — fine for a marketing site.
- [ ] Domain: `protocol.chalkagents.com` CNAME → Vercel; `chalk-protocol-site.vercel.app` until DNS is ready.
- [ ] After the domain is live: set `chalkagents/chalk-protocol`'s repo `homepage` field and add the link to its README (small PR in the parent repo).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Production deploys only from pushes to main; PR previews require member approval for forks.
- [ ] No env vars / secrets in this repo or exposed to previews.
- [ ] Site reachable at the production URL.

block.reason: Vercel dashboard toggles only the operator can flip: main-only production, fork-deployment authorization, zero env vars (see issue #5 comment — deploy is already live)

title: chore(security): branch-protect main — PR + required test check, squash-only, Dependabot

acceptanceCriteria.text: Direct push to main is rejected; PRs without a green `test` check cannot merge.
`chalk pipeline` still lands an issue-backed task end-to-end under protection (squash via PR is unaffected).
Dependabot security alerts enabled.

issue.body:
## Goal

Main is the only path to production, and nothing lands on it outside the gated loop. Mirrors the parent repo's posture.

## Scope (GitHub settings — human/gh-api work, chalk task will block needs:human-input and that's fine)

- Branch protection on `main`: PR required (0 approvals — the adversarial reviewer is the approval), required status check `test` (needs the CI issue landed first), enforce for admins, no force-push, no deletion.
- Merge methods: squash only (matches `github.mergeMethod` in the spine).
- Enable Dependabot security updates (lockfile alerts for `astro`).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Direct push to main is rejected; PRs without a green `test` check cannot merge.
- [ ] `chalk pipeline` still lands an issue-backed task end-to-end under protection (squash via PR is unaffected).
- [ ] Dependabot security alerts enabled.

Depends on: the CI verify lane issue.

title: ci: GitHub Actions verify lane — node --test on PRs + main (remote broke-check for the merge gate)

acceptanceCriteria.text: CI runs on PRs and on pushes to main; green on the current main.
Workflow declares explicit read-only `permissions` and uses no secrets.
`chalk merge`'s broke-check reports source=remote-CI (not local fallback) on the next issue-backed PR.

issue.body:
## Goal

A GitHub Actions verify lane so the protocol's merge gate gets a real remote broke-check (today it falls back to local verify), and so branch protection has a required check to enforce.

## Scope

- `.github/workflows/test.yml`: on `pull_request` and `push` to `main` — checkout, setup-node (LTS), `npm ci`, `node --test` (the locked test installs nothing extra and runs the real Astro build).
- **Least privilege, fork-safe**: top-level `permissions: contents: read`; plain `pull_request` trigger only — never `pull_request_target`; no secrets referenced anywhere in the workflow.
- Name the job `test` so it can be the required status check.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] CI runs on PRs and on pushes to main; green on the current main.
- [ ] Workflow declares explicit read-only `permissions` and uses no secrets.
- [ ] `chalk merge`'s broke-check reports source=remote-CI (not local fallback) on the next issue-backed PR.

findings.note: Criteria 1 ('green on the current main') and 3 (broke-check reports source=remote-CI) are only observable after this PR merges and the workflow actually runs; nothing in-repo can prove them. The workflow content is the correct minimal enabler: `npm test` -> `node --test` exercises both test files (build.test.mjs self-installs/builds and CI runs `npm ci` first with a lockfile present, Node 22 satisfies Astro 5), so a red run on main is unlikely, but confirm the first Actions run and the next `chalk merge` broke-check output before considering criterion 3 discharged.

test/ci-workflow.test.mjs asserts the workflow's triggers, read-only permissions, absence of secrets, and npm ci/test steps, and every assertion fails if the workflow is reverted — the change is genuinely covered. However the regexes are format-brittle: a future multi-line `branches:\n  - main` list would fail the push-trigger test, and `doesNotMatch(/:\s*write\b/)` can false-positive on unrelated YAML values (e.g., a step named 'mode: write'). Acceptable now, may cause spurious failures on benign refactors.

The permissions test only requires a top-level `permissions: contents: read`; a job-level `permissions:` block adding scopes would still satisfy the first two assertions (the `:write` guard would catch write grants but not added non-read scopes like `contents: read` + `issues: read`). Current workflow has no job-level block, so criterion 2 is met as shipped.
Adding .github/workflows/ci.yml and a new test file touches no existing source or tests; `npm test` gains one fast file-content test. No plausible regression to existing behavior.

plan:
# Plan — GitHub Actions verify lane

**Approach:** Add a single minimal workflow `.github/workflows/ci.yml` that runs `npm ci` + `npm test` (`node --test`, which itself installs/builds via `test/build.test.mjs`) on PRs and pushes to `main`, plus a locked-style node test asserting the workflow's shape. Alternative (separate build + test jobs) rejected — one job mirrors `chalk verify` exactly and keeps the diff small. No YAML parser dep is allowed (`build.test.mjs` locks deps to `astro` only), so the new test asserts via string/regex on the raw YAML.

**Steps:**
1. Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
   - `name: CI`; `on: { pull_request: {}, push: { branches: [main] } }`
   - Top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` — nothing else; no `secrets.*` references anywhere.
   - One job `test` on `ubuntu-latest`: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4` (node `22`, `cache: npm`), `npm ci`, `npm test`.
2. Add `test/ci-workflow.test.mjs` (node:test) reading the YAML as text and asserting: file exists; triggers include `pull_request` and `push` to `main`; declares `permissions:` with `contents: read` and no `write`; contains no `secrets.` reference; runs `npm test`.
3. Run `chalk verify` — `node --test` picks up both test files; loop to GREEN.
4. Criterion 3 (broke-check `source=remote-CI`) is satisfied automatically once this workflow exists on the repo — no code needed; note it in `chalk decision`/`chalk update`.

**Files:**
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (new)
- `test/ci-workflow.test.mjs` (new; mirror path/style of existing `test/build.test.mjs`)

**Test:** `test/ci-workflow.test.mjs` — fails today (workflow file absent), passes once `ci.yml` exists with PR+main triggers, read-only `permissions`, and no secrets.

title: design: professional developer-centric UI/UX pass — landing hierarchy, terminal motif, real docs chrome

acceptanceCriteria.text: Landing page: above-the-fold hero with install + CTAs, terminal-motif loop section, gates/quickstart/docs sections with consistent styling.
Docs pages: styled markdown (incl. Shiki code blocks), heading anchors, prev/next nav, consistent header/footer chrome.
OG/social meta + favicon on every page.
All existing locked tests pass unmodified; new/changed presentation covered by at least one new test (e.g. docs chrome present on all three docs pages).

issue.body:
## Problem

The scaffold page is functional but generic. For a tool whose whole pitch is engineering rigor, the page itself is evidence — it should look like it was designed by people who care about developer experience: strong typography, terminal-native aesthetics, skimmable structure, and docs pages that feel like real docs rather than dumped markdown.

## Direction (professional, developer-centric)

**Landing page**
- Above the fold: pitch + install command + CTAs visible without scrolling on a laptop; tighter visual hierarchy (type scale, spacing rhythm, max-width for prose).
- A terminal-window motif for the loop section — render the read → work → verify → write loop as a styled terminal session (`$ chalk verify` → RED → fix → GREEN), static markup, no JS. This is the product's money shot: the gate refusing.
- Section rhythm: loop → gates (the four levers as cards) → quickstart → docs. Each section skimmable in one glance.
- Real code/pre styling: syntax-tinted, consistent with the terminal motif (Astro's built-in Shiki is available without new deps).

**Docs pages (/protocol, /quickstart, /research)**
- Readable measure (~70ch), heading anchor links, a small table of contents for long docs, prev/next footer nav between the three docs, "edit on GitHub" per page.
- Style the rendered markdown properly: code blocks (Shiki), tables, blockquotes, lists — currently browser-default.

**Site chrome**
- Consistent header nav (logo/wordmark, Docs, GitHub, npm) and footer across all pages.
- OG/social meta: og:title/description/image (a simple generated OG card is fine), favicon, meta description.
- Accessibility: visible focus states, sufficient contrast in the dark theme, semantic landmarks, `prefers-reduced-motion` respected if anything animates.

## Constraints

- **The locked content contract stays**: `test/site.test.mjs` and `test/docs-pages.test.mjs` are LOCKED — the pitch line, install command, loop steps, quickstart, docs links, and docs rendering behavior must survive the redesign verbatim. Presentation changes, content contract doesn't.
- No new runtime dependencies beyond `astro` (Shiki ships inside Astro). Zero client-side JS stays the default; if a copy-to-clipboard button is wanted, it's a few lines of inline vanilla JS, not a framework.
- Performance: the page should stay effectively static — no layout shift, instant paint.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Landing page: above-the-fold hero with install + CTAs, terminal-motif loop section, gates/quickstart/docs sections with consistent styling.
- [ ] Docs pages: styled markdown (incl. Shiki code blocks), heading anchors, prev/next nav, consistent header/footer chrome.
- [ ] OG/social meta + favicon on every page.
- [ ] All existing locked tests pass unmodified; new/changed presentation covered by at least one new test (e.g. docs chrome present on all three docs pages).

findings.note: astro.config.mjs imports '@astrojs/markdown-remark' which is NOT a declared dependency — it resolves only because npm hoists it as a transitive dep of astro (the locked build test pins dependencies to ['astro'], so it cannot be declared). This is a phantom-dependency: a future astro minor/patch that re-exports or drops @astrojs/markdown-remark hoisting, or a switch to a strict installer (pnpm), breaks every build. Consider importing rehypeHeadingIds via an astro-exported path or documenting the coupling.

OG meta omits og:image and twitter:card is 'summary' with no image, so social cards render text-only. The criterion ('OG/social meta') is arguably satisfied, but a share preview without an image is a weak social card for a launch page.

The terminal-motif and chrome assertions match literal strings like class="terminal" / class="doc-pager"; they hold under Astro's default scopedStyleStrategy 'attribute' but would break spuriously if the strategy changed to class-based scoping — brittle, though currently correct.

site-chrome fixture server answers ANY *.md request with the same fixture, so it cannot detect the loader fetching the wrong file per slug; the pre-existing docs-pages test covers that, so exposure is minimal.

heading-anchors deliberately skips h1 to preserve the locked docs-pages assertion `${heading}` — verified compatible; rehypeHeadingIds still adds the h1 id before the closing tag position asserted, so no locked-test conflict.

plan: # Plan — professional developer-centric UI/UX pass **Approach:** Hand-rolled shared layout + scoped CSS inside `src/` (no new packages — the locked test `build.test.mjs` pins `dependencies === ['astro']`, `devDependencies === {}`, so Tailwind/rehype-slug are off the table). Astro already ships Shiki and `rehypeHeadingIds` by default; heading-anchor links come from a tiny custom rehype plugin in `src/`. Both test sandboxes copy only `src`, `public`, `astro.config.mjs`, `package.json`, `tsconfig.json` — all shared code must live there. **Steps:** 1. Add `src/layouts/Base.astro`: shared `` (title/description props, ``, `og:title/og:description/og:url/og:type/og:site_name`, `twitter:card`, canonical via `Astro.site`), site header (logo → `/`, links to docs + repo), footer, and the global design tokens/styles (dark terminal-leaning palette, mono accents). 2. Add `public/favicon.svg` (simple chalk-mark glyph). 3. Add `src/lib/heading-anchors.mjs`: rehype plugin appending `` to **h2–h6 only** — h1 must stay flush so `docs-pages.test.mjs`'s `` `${heading}` `` assertion keeps passing. Wire it plus `shikiConfig: { themes: { light: 'github-light', dark: 'github-dark' } }` into `astro.config.mjs` markdown config (the content-layer `renderMarkdown` honors it). 4. Export `DOCS` from `src/content.config.ts`; in `src/pages/[slug].astro` compute prev/next from that order, add docs chrome (Base layout, source link, prev/next nav, styled `article` incl. `.astro-code` blocks, anchor hover styles). 5. Redesign `src/pages/index.astro` on Base layout: above-the-fold hero (keep the exact locked strings — pitch lines, `>npm install -g chalk-protocol<` as element text, npm/repo hrefs), loop section restyled as a terminal window (keep the four gate names verbatim), quickstart (keep the six `chalk *` commands), docs grid (keep local `/slug` hrefs and the four `blob/main/*.md` source links). 6. Add `test/site-chrome.test.mjs` (clone the sandbox + fixture-server pattern from `test/npm-package.test.mjs`, fixture markdown includes a fenced code block). **Files:** `src/layouts/Base.astro` (new), `src/lib/heading-anchors.mjs` (new), `public/favicon.svg` (new), `astro.config.mjs`, `src/content.config.ts`, `src/pages/index.astro`, `src/pages/[slug].astro`, `test/site-chrome.test.mjs` (new). **Test (fails today, passes after):** `test/site-chrome.test.mjs` — sandbox build, then assert on `dist/index.html` **and all three** `dist/{protocol,quickstart,research}/index.html`: `rel="icon"` favicon link and `property="og:title"` meta present; shared header/footer markers (e.g. `

title: feat: reference the npm package — install command in the hero + link to npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol

acceptanceCriteria.text: The built landing page contains `npm install -g chalk-protocol` as visible, selectable text.
The page links to https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol.
New locked-style test covers both; existing locked tests untouched and green.

issue.body:
## Problem

Chalk Protocol is on npm — https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol (`chalk-protocol@0.1.0`, installs the `chalk` bin) — but the landing page never mentions it. The hero's calls-to-action are "Get started" and "View the repo"; a developer who wants to try it has to leave for GitHub to find the install command. The single highest-intent action on a dev-tool landing page is copying the install line.

## Scope

- Hero: a copy-pasteable install block — `npm install -g chalk-protocol` — styled as a terminal line, above the fold next to the CTAs.
- An explicit npm link (text link or badge) pointing at https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol — hero or footer, wherever it reads naturally alongside the GitHub link.
- Footer: add npm alongside the existing GitHub link.

## Constraints

- `test/site.test.mjs` is LOCKED (task-3ce754f). Do not edit it — cover the new content with a new test file (e.g. `test/npm-reference.test.mjs`) asserting the built page contains the install command and the npm package URL. If a locked assertion genuinely must change, that's `chalk amend-spec`, a human step.
- No new dependencies; keep the zero-client-JS property.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The built landing page contains `npm install -g chalk-protocol` as visible, selectable text.
- [ ] The page links to https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol.
- [ ] New locked-style test covers both; existing locked tests untouched and green.

findings.note: The visibility assertion (/>npm install -g chalk-protocol</) proves the string is element text in dist/index.html, but does not pin it to the hero section as the task title specifies ('install command in the hero'); the test would still pass if the snippet migrated to the footer. The written acceptance criterion #1 only requires 'the built landing page contains' it, so this is a looseness against the task intent, not the contract. Both tests fail if the diff is reverted, so coverage is non-vacuous.

The link assertion checks only that href="https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol" appears somewhere in the HTML; it does not verify it is on an <a> element or that the anchor has visible text. In practice the diff renders it as a visible 'View on npm' CTA button, and the criterion #2 wording ('the page links to') is satisfied.

node --test runs test files in parallel; three concurrent Astro builds (root build.test.mjs, docs-pages sandbox, npm-package sandbox) share node_modules via symlink and could contend on shared caches (e.g. node_modules/.astro). The sandbox-copy pattern mirrors the pre-existing docs-pages.test.mjs approach and avoids the dist/ race, so risk is pre-existing and not worsened materially. Existing locked tests (build.test.mjs, docs-pages.test.mjs, ci-workflow.test.mjs) are untouched by the diff, satisfying criterion #3.

The install command is rendered in <pre><code> with normal CSS (no display:none, no user-select:none), so it is visible and selectable; the npm URL constant matches the required https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol exactly.

plan:
**Approach:** Add the install command + npm link to the hero in `src/pages/index.astro`, and cover both in a new test file that builds in an isolated tmp sandbox (the `test/docs-pages.test.mjs` pattern) — extending `test/build.test.mjs` is off the table (locked; criterion 3), and a second root `npm run build` would race build.test.mjs on `dist/` under `node --test` parallelism. **Steps:** 1. In `src/pages/index.astro` frontmatter, add `const npm = 'https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk-protocol';`. 2. In the `.hero` section, add an install snippet as real text, e.g. `
npm install -g chalk-protocol
` (plain markup ⇒ visible, selectable), plus a small style rule matching the existing `.steps code` look. 3. Add a link to the CTA row: `View on npm`. 4. Create `test/npm-package.test.mjs`: copy the docs-pages sandbox recipe — `mkdtempSync`, copy `src`/`public`/`astro.config.mjs`/`package.json`, symlink `node_modules`, run `npm run build` in the sandbox (async or sync is fine here; no fixture server needed), read `sandbox/dist/index.html`. 5. Assertions: (a) install command rendered as element text — `assert.match(html, />npm install -g chalk-protocol

title: design: redesign v2 — full terminal brutalist (CRT phosphor, animated gate-refusal hero, everything is a session)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Landing page is the terminal-brutalist design: hero terminal with the animated gate-refusal session, CRT treatment, terminal-framed sections, status-bar footer.
All locked tests pass unmodified; site-chrome coverage maintained or extended (e.g. animation is reduced-motion-safe, copy button present).
Docs pages match the aesthetic without losing v1 chrome (anchors, prev/next, source links).
No new deps; client JS limited to the copy interaction.

issue.body:
## Direction (decided with the maintainer)

Redesign v2: **full terminal brutalist**. The site IS a terminal session — CRT phosphor aesthetic, the gate refusing an agent in a typed animation. charm.sh / terminal.shop energy. This replaces the v1 "safe dark theme" (#12/PR #15), which was competent but generic.

## Design spec

- **Whole page monospace** on near-black; green phosphor primary, amber accent; subtle CRT texture (scanlines via CSS gradient overlay, soft glow text-shadow, vignette). All motion respects `prefers-reduced-motion`.
- **Hero = a terminal window** playing the money shot as a CSS typing animation: `chalk done` → `✗ GATE P4: verify RED — not your call.` → fix → `✓ GREEN` → `✓ done — the gate decided, not the model.` Blinking block cursor.
- Pitch line rendered as display type (uppercase via CSS `text-transform`; the source text stays the exact locked string).
- **Install block**: `[ npm install -g chalk-protocol ]` with a copy button (a few lines of inline vanilla JS — the only JS on the page).
- **Every section framed as terminal output**: gates as `$ chalk gates` output rows, quickstart as a session transcript, docs as an `ls`-style listing. tmux-style status-bar footer with npm / GitHub links.
- **Docs pages** restyled to match: terminal frame chrome, phosphor markdown styling; keep heading anchors + prev/next from v1.

## Constraints

- LOCKED (do not edit): `test/site.test.mjs`, `test/docs-pages.test.mjs`. The content contract survives verbatim: pitch line, loop steps, quickstart, install command, npm + docs links, docs rendering behavior.
- `test/site-chrome.test.mjs` may be adapted (not locked) but its coverage level must not drop.
- No new dependencies; static output; JS budget = the copy button only.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Landing page is the terminal-brutalist design: hero terminal with the animated gate-refusal session, CRT treatment, terminal-framed sections, status-bar footer.
- [ ] All locked tests pass unmodified; site-chrome coverage maintained or extended (e.g. animation is reduced-motion-safe, copy button present).
- [ ] Docs pages match the aesthetic without losing v1 chrome (anchors, prev/next, source links).
- [ ] No new deps; client JS limited to the copy interaction.

findings.note: Base.astro adds a global `a:hover { background: var(--amber); color:#0a0700 }` while remapping --accent to amber. Astro scopes page styles with :where() (specificity 0), so on docs pages the pager cards (.pager-link, background (0,1,0)) lose to global a:hover (0,1,1): hovering a prev/next pager card paints it amber, but the nested .pager-title span keeps its own color: var(--accent) — now amber — yielding amber-on-amber (invisible) text. Same hazard for .doc-meta links styled with their own colors. This degrades v1 docs chrome, violating criterion 3, and no test exercises hover/contrast.

The 'reduced-motion-safe' test only asserts the substring 'prefers-reduced-motion' appears anywhere in the built HTML. Base.astro's brand-mark blink guard alone satisfies it — the test would still pass if the hero session's typing/fade animations were NOT gated (e.g. if the `no-preference` wrapper in index.astro were removed). It does not verify that the gate-refusal session itself is motion-safe, which is the behavior criterion 2 names.

Criterion 1 explicitly requires a 'status-bar footer'. The footer was rewritten into a tmux-style status bar (.status-bar, seg-* spans, new npm link) but the only footer assertion is the pre-existing `/<footer[^>]*>/` check — every footer test passes if the status-bar redesign is reverted. Shipped behavior with no covering assertion.

Criterion 3 says docs pages 'match the aesthetic'. src/pages/[slug].astro was untouched: it still applies v1 rounded chrome (article pre border-radius: 0.6rem — which, as `article :where(...) pre`, overrides Base's new `.astro-code { border-radius: 0 }`; plus rounded inline code, blockquotes, pager cards) directly contradicting the declared brutalist 'radius 0' rule. Docs only inherit the palette/mono font by accident of CSS variables; no test asserts docs match the new aesthetic.

Criterion 4 ('no new deps; client JS limited to the copy interaction') is unasserted: no test checks package.json dependencies or that the built landing page ships only the copy script. package.json is currently unchanged, but nothing gates it.

The typing animation uses a hard-coded `steps(24, end)` for every command regardless of length (--n varies 11–41 chars), so 'typing' cadence doesn't match per-character reveal; cosmetic only. Also the copy button restores `btn.textContent = prev` — if a user double-clicks within 1200ms the saved `prev` can be '[ copied ✓ ]', permanently sticking the label.

Hero session command lines use `.line .txt { white-space: pre; overflow: hidden; max-width: 100% }` — the longest typed line ('vim src/gate.mjs        # fix it for real', 41ch) can be permanently clipped with no wrap, scroll, or ellipsis on narrow viewports (~<380px), for both animated and reduced-motion users. No test covers narrow-viewport rendering of the session.

The reduced-motion test is heuristic: it only inspects a fixed 6000-char window after the FIRST no-preference media query and only forbids `opacity:0` before it. Hiding via `visibility:hidden`, `width:0`, or an `opacity:0` placed after the guard-but-outside-it would pass. In particular, the brand-mark blink (`visibility:hidden` keyframe in Base.astro) relies on a separate `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` guard that NO test asserts — deleting that guard would keep the suite green.

The 'client JS is the copy interaction only' test counts script tags on the landing page only; docs pages are unchecked, so a future script added to Base/[slug] would not violate the criterion-4 test. Also the copy test only asserts the data-copy attribute and the word 'clipboard' — the double-click label-restore behavior specifically called out in the code comment is untested.

`inlineStylesheets: 'always'` inlines the full CSS bundle into every page (weight increase, no cross-page CSS caching) and the reduced-motion test is now coupled to this build setting — reverting it to the default would break the test for an unrelated reason. Documented trade-off, acceptable for a 4-page site.

The '[slug].astro' comment declares 'no rounded corners anywhere — everything is a readout', but the landing terminal dots use `border-radius: 50%`, and the no-radius test deliberately excludes the landing page. Internally inconsistent with the stated rule, though not a breach of the written acceptance criteria.

The hero session container is a <div> with aria-label but no role; most assistive tech ignores aria-label on generic divs, and the timed reveal means the full refusal narrative is in the DOM immediately (good) but the label itself likely isn't announced. Minor a11y polish, untested.

title: fix: verify gate is availability-coupled — root-build tests live-fetch upstream markdown and transient network failures turn verify RED

issue.body:
Twice today the verify gate went RED on a transient fetch failure (undici timeout against raw.githubusercontent.com) and was green on immediate re-run — once at the root checkout, once blocking the #12 pipeline run mid-sweep. The docs loader's fail-hard behavior is correct for production builds (#6 criterion 2), but it makes every local `chalk verify` and CI run a bet on GitHub raw availability, and a flaky gate erodes trust in RED.

Options to evaluate (pick in the task):
- Bounded retry with backoff in the loader for transient errors (timeouts/5xx — NOT 404s, which must stay fail-hard).
- Point the root-build test at the local fixture server (like test/docs-pages.test.mjs already does) so the deterministic suite is network-free, and keep one explicitly-marked live smoke test for the real upstream default.
- Both.

Acceptance: `npm test` is deterministic offline except at most one clearly-labeled live test; production build behavior (fail-hard on bad fetch) unchanged; locked tests amended only via `chalk amend-spec` if their assertions must move to fixtures.

Found while dogfooding — the reviewer flagged this exact exposure as a low finding on PR #9.

title: design: v2.1 modern terminal — keep the session identity, calm the surface for readability

acceptanceCriteria.text: Prose renders in sans, sentence case; mono only for terminal/command content.
Scanlines/glow scoped to terminal frames; no texture overlay on body text.
Section headings + eyebrow pattern; hero readable at a glance (pitch, install, session).
Full suite green; locked tests unmodified.

issue.body:
## Direction (maintainer feedback on v2)

Keep the terminal identity — the animated gate-refusal hero, ASCII wordmark, session framing all stay. But v2 reads as a CRT cosplay: full-page scanlines, all-mono all-caps prose, and glow on body text tax readability. v2.1 = **modern terminal**: the terminal lives in the terminals; the prose around them is clean, calm, and instantly scannable.

## Spec

- **Typography split**: prose (pitch, descriptions, docs body) in a clean system sans; mono reserved for terminal content, commands, code, and labels. Pitch in sentence case at display size — no all-caps paragraphs.
- **Calm the surface**: scanline/vignette texture only INSIDE `.terminal` frames, never over reading text. Text glow only on terminal/accent elements. Body text near-white on the dark green-black ground, comfortable contrast.
- **Hierarchy for grasp-ability**: real sans headings ("The loop", "Quickstart", "Docs") with the `$ chalk …` motif as a small mono eyebrow above each; generous whitespace; prose measure ≤ 42rem.
- **Gates easier to scan**: keep the terminal frame, but gate names in mono + descriptions in sans with breathing room.
- Docs pages follow: sans body, mono code/chrome, same calm surface.
- Keep: hero session animation (reduced-motion-safe), copy button, status-bar footer, no-radius rule, one-script budget, zero new deps.

## Constraints

- LOCKED: `test/docs-pages.test.mjs` untouched; all contract strings survive (pitch, gates, steps, links, install).
- `test/site-chrome.test.mjs` adaptable, coverage must not drop.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Prose renders in sans, sentence case; mono only for terminal/command content.
- [ ] Scanlines/glow scoped to terminal frames; no texture overlay on body text.
- [ ] Section headings + eyebrow pattern; hero readable at a glance (pitch, install, session).
- [ ] Full suite green; locked tests unmodified.

findings.note: Zero tests cover any of the changed behavior. Criteria 1–3 (sans prose, scanlines scoped to .terminal, eyebrow+heading pattern) have no assertions anywhere: test/site-chrome.test.mjs only checks pre-existing markup (class="terminal", session strings, copy button, reduced-motion guard, footer links), none of which this diff touches. Reverting the entire diff would leave the full suite green — a vacuous pass. The task's locked-test list is '(none)', so the change ships design behavior with no test that would catch a regression (e.g., --sans being re-aliased to --mono, or body::before scanlines reappearing globally).

Criterion 1 says 'Prose renders in sans, sentence case; mono only for terminal/command content', but non-terminal prose/UI remains mono and/or uppercase: the h1 .wordmark is mono + text-transform:uppercase (index.astro), .doc-meta nav links and doc-links anchors are forced mono ([slug].astro:59, index.astro:517), the docs pager labels are uppercase ([slug].astro:217), and the entire header nav and footer are mono. Some of this is defensible as 'terminal chrome', but the criterion as written draws the line at terminal/command content, and doc titles like 'README — What Chalk Protocol is...' links and pager labels are navigation prose, not commands. The contract is ambiguous enough that this needed an explicit decision, not silent inclusion.

Scanline overlay (.terminal::after, position:absolute, no z-index) sits in the same stacking level as the animated .session .line elements (opacity animations create stacking contexts with z-index:0). Paint order currently favors the ::after because it is last in tree order, but this is fragile — any future z-index on session content silently lifts text above the scanlines or vice versa. An explicit z-index on the overlay would make the scoping robust.

The global vignette (old body::after inset box-shadow) was removed along with the global scanlines, and the body radial gradient endpoint color changed (#0a1f0e→#0b1a0f). No test guards the visual identity ('keep the session identity' per the task title); .install-cmd keeps text-shadow glow while .steps code lost its glow — inconsistent phosphor treatment across command content with nothing asserting which is intended.

Criterion 4 ('full suite green') cannot be independently confirmed from the diff alone; even if green, the suite's brutalist no-radius regex and chrome checks are the only style-level assertions and they predate this change — no new assertion was added for v2.1 despite the task being entirely about observable rendered output that is testable in built HTML/CSS (e.g., asserting body font-family resolves to the sans stack, or that repeating-linear-gradient appears only within a .terminal-scoped rule).

The three v2.1 tests are genuinely revert-sensitive for criteria 1–3 (--sans re-aliased to mono, body-scoped scanlines, or eyebrow/heading collapse would each fail), but they under-specify the contract: nothing asserts headings lost their uppercase/phosphor-glow styling, that the eyebrow itself renders mono, or that the vignette (body::after inset box-shadow) stays removed — a partial revert re-adding `text-transform:uppercase`/`text-shadow:var(--glow)` to h2 or a page-global vignette overlay would still pass green.

Criterion 2 says scanlines/GLOW scoped to terminal frames, yet glow text-shadows remain outside frames: .brand in the site header, .wordmark (amber glow), .ascii hero art, and the docs pager hover (.pager-link:hover .pager-title gets var(--glow)). These are chrome/brand/command elements so they arguably honor the design intent, but they are not literally inside .terminal frames.

Sentence-case criterion: .gates strong keeps text-transform:uppercase (defensible — it lives inside a terminal frame) and the wordmark stays uppercase (brand mark). No prose paragraph retains uppercase, so criterion 1 holds in substance.

The .terminal::after scanline overlay now sits above terminal content with z-index:1; pointer-events:none prevents interaction problems and the darkened stripe opacity was reduced (0.22→0.16), and no other test file asserts the old palette, body::before overlay, or mono body, so no existing assertion regresses.

Scanline scoping and sans-body assertions run only against the landing page HTML; acceptable because the styles are global in Base.astro and inlined identically on doc pages, but a doc-page-scoped scanline overlay added later would go undetected.

title: design: docs three-column upgrade — left nav + sticky TOC, code-copy buttons, callouts (hand-rolled, zero-dep)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Docs pages render a three-column layout: left doc nav (active highlighted), constrained prose, right sticky TOC of the page's headings.
Every code block has a working copy button (hand-rolled, no dep).
GitHub-alert callouts in the source markdown render as styled note/tip/warning/caution boxes.
Prose column is width-constrained and readable; prev/next, source link, heading anchors retained.
All locked tests pass unmodified; new chrome covered by revert-sensitive assertions in test/site-chrome.test.mjs.
Zero new deps; full suite green.

issue.body:
## Direction

Research-backed docs upgrade (comparables: Starlight, VitePress, Nextra, Bun, Prisma, Tailwind, tRPC). The 3 docs pages (/protocol, /quickstart, /research) render long single markdown documents with only a prev/next pager — no navigation, no TOC, no code-copy. Move to the universal three-column docs skeleton, hand-rolled (zero new deps — maintainer decision; Pagefind search deliberately skipped, low ROI on 3 pages).

## Add / change (priority order)

1. **Three-column layout**: persistent left nav (the 3 docs, current one highlighted) / constrained prose column (~70ch) / sticky right-hand "On this page" TOC built from the H2/H3 headings we already parse, with an IntersectionObserver active-section highlight. Collapse both rails responsibly on narrow screens.
2. **Copy button on every code block** — hand-rolled: a tiny rehype pass (like src/lib/heading-anchors.mjs) wraps Shiki `<pre>` with a copy button + a small vanilla-JS handler. No dependency.
3. **Callouts / admonitions** — render GitHub-alert syntax (`> [!NOTE] / [!TIP] / [!WARNING] / [!CAUTION]`) from the parent markdown as colored boxes, via a hand-rolled rehype function. Portable because it lives in the source markdown.
4. **Constrain content width + readable typography** on the prose column (sans body from v2.1, ~70ch, comfortable line-height).
5. Keep the existing prev/next pager, source link, heading anchors, and Shiki blocks.

## Constraints

- LOCKED (do not edit): test/docs-pages.test.mjs — docs still render the parent repo's current markdown at build time; that behavior and its assertions must hold.
- No new runtime deps (dependencies stays exactly [astro]); TOC active-highlight + copy = tiny vanilla JS.
- Keep v2.1 identity (sans prose, mono terminal chrome, scanlines only in .terminal, reduced-motion-safe) and existing site-chrome assertions.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Docs pages render a three-column layout: left doc nav (active highlighted), constrained prose, right sticky TOC of the page's headings.
- [ ] Every code block has a working copy button (hand-rolled, no dep).
- [ ] GitHub-alert callouts in the source markdown render as styled note/tip/warning/caution boxes.
- [ ] Prose column is width-constrained and readable; prev/next, source link, heading anchors retained.
- [ ] All locked tests pass unmodified; new chrome covered by revert-sensitive assertions in test/site-chrome.test.mjs.
- [ ] Zero new deps; full suite green.

findings.note: Criterion 3 (GitHub-alert callouts render in the site) is not covered by any revert-sensitive integration test. callouts.test.mjs only exercises the transform against synthetic hast trees; the build fixture (site-chrome.test.mjs) contains no `> [!NOTE]` markdown. Removing `callouts` from astro.config.mjs's rehypePlugins array — i.e. reverting the actual site wiring that satisfies the criterion — leaves the entire suite green. The end-to-end 'renders as styled boxes in the source markdown' deliverable is effectively unasserted.

No doc in the corpus uses GitHub-alert syntax and no rendered-page assertion exists, so it is unverified that the real remark→rehype pipeline yields a single first `

` text node beginning with `[!NOTE]` (soft-break/multi-child paragraph handling, marker split across nodes). The plugin's assumption is proven only on hand-built trees, not the actual Astro output, so criterion 3's real rendering path is untested.

The 'working copy button' (criterion 2) and the active-TOC IntersectionObserver behavior are only asserted structurally (presence of .code-block/.code-copy/.toc-link). The clipboard click handler and scroll-spy JS are never executed by any test, so 'working' is unproven; a broken handler would still pass.

The copy-button 'working' criterion (2) and the TOC scroll-spy active-state are only asserted by string presence of 'clipboard'/'IntersectionObserver' in the built HTML, not by executing the click handler or the IntersectionObserver logic. The rehype markup and JS presence are revert-sensitive, but the actual clipboard write and active-highlight branch (seen set / rootMargin) are never behaviorally exercised. Acceptable given headless-build constraints and simple handlers, but the runtime behavior is unverified.

Callout marker stripping relies on remark emitting the alert marker and body as a single hast text node containing '[!NOTE]\n...'. If a soft break ever produces a separate text/break node, the marker match would fail; the end-to-end fixture covers the common case only. Minor edge risk, currently green.

Criterion 4's 'source link retained' has no dedicated assertion in the added chrome tests (only prev/next and heading anchors are asserted); the doc-source markup is retained in code but not guarded by a revert-sensitive test.

title: design: landing reframe — name the enemy, feature grid, before/after, honest trust strip, real footer

acceptanceCriteria.text: Hero leads with a category headline + an enemy/problem line; the existing pitch + install + session are retained.
A feature grid names all four levers, each linking to a doc.
A before/after (without/with Chalk) section is present.
An honest "works with any agent" trust strip is present; no fabricated metrics.
A real footer with Docs + Community link columns.
All locked tests pass unmodified; new sections covered by new assertions in test/site-chrome.test.mjs (revert-sensitive, per the reviewer's standing bar).
Zero new deps; full suite green.

issue.body:
## Direction

Research-backed landing reframe (comparables: esbuild, biome, bun, atuin, opencode, turborepo, linear). Today the page states what Chalk *is* but never argues *why* or shows *any* trust signal. Keep the best existing assets — animated gate-refusal session, ASCII wordmark, v2.1 modern-terminal styling — and add the missing argument + proof. Zero new deps (maintainer decision); honest trust signals only (repo has 0 stars — NO fabricated numbers).

## Add / change (priority order)

1. **Category headline + "name the enemy" line** above the install. New h1-scale positioning line (e.g. "The quality gate for AI coding agents") and a one-liner naming the failure Chalk prevents ("AI agents will tell you they're done. Chalk makes them prove it."). The existing pitch line stays as the sub-pitch.
2. **Feature grid — the four levers.** A 4-card grid after the hero: Verify / Locked tests / Adversarial review / Merge gate, each = name + one-line tagline + link to the relevant doc. (biome/bun/zed pattern.)
3. **Before/after comparison.** Two columns: "Agent without Chalk" (self-declares done, tests deleted, bug ships) vs "Agent with Chalk" (verify refuses, review blocks, task can't close). Makes the value legible without watching the animation.
4. **Honest trust strip.** "Works with any agent — Claude Code · Cursor · opencode · aider" + "built through chalk itself" (already true/provable). No star counts.
5. **Loop as a numbered walkthrough** (1..N) with the real commands (chalk next → context → start → verify → done), replacing/augmenting the bare gate list.
6. **Real 2-column footer** (Docs / Community + social + license), replacing the status-bar-only footer. Keep it small — no funded-company four-column wall.

## Constraints

- LOCKED (do not edit): test/docs-pages.test.mjs. The locked landing strings MUST survive verbatim: "Ship agent-written code through gates, not vibes", "a locked spec decides success, not the model", the four gate names, the quickstart command steps, the four parent-doc links, "npm install -g chalk-protocol".
- No new runtime deps (dependencies stays exactly [astro]); client JS stays limited (copy button; any new interaction must be tiny vanilla JS).
- Keep v2.1 identity: sans prose, mono for terminal content, scanlines only inside .terminal, reduced-motion-safe.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Hero leads with a category headline + an enemy/problem line; the existing pitch + install + session are retained.
- [ ] A feature grid names all four levers, each linking to a doc.
- [ ] A before/after (without/with Chalk) section is present.
- [ ] An honest "works with any agent" trust strip is present; no fabricated metrics.
- [ ] A real footer with Docs + Community link columns.
- [ ] All locked tests pass unmodified; new sections covered by new assertions in test/site-chrome.test.mjs (revert-sensitive, per the reviewer's standing bar).
- [ ] Zero new deps; full suite green.

findings.note: Criterion 4 requires 'no fabricated metrics' but no assertion negatively guards against a future fabricated stat being added; the current strip is honest (agent names only), so this is acceptable but the contract's negative clause is unguarded.

Criterion 1 also mandates the install command and hero session are retained; the new tests assert only the pitch line's retention. Both are present and untouched in the diff, so no regression, but their retention is not revert-sensitive.

Feature-grid links point to /protocol (x3) and /quickstart — all valid built doc pages per the pages array; the 'each linking to a doc' criterion is met, though the test only asserts one lever-link class exists rather than one per card (all four are rendered via map, so covered).
